attrs.alt: Conditional Access policy structure: signal conditions feed into evaluation engine producing Block, MFA challenge, device enrollment, or access with session controls

content.text: A Conditional Access policy is an if-then rule evaluated at sign-in time: if a set of conditions match (user, application, location, device state, risk level), then apply a set of controls (block, require MFA, require compliant device, apply session restrictions). Every enabled policy that matches a sign-in is evaluated — there is no first-match-wins short-circuit. All matching policy results are combined, and the most restrictive outcome wins: a Block from any policy overrides all Grant controls.

Conditions define who the policy applies to: specific users or groups, specific applications, specific client app types (browser vs native client), locations (named locations or any location), device state, sign-in risk, and user risk.

Grant controls define what happens when conditions match: block, require MFA, require compliant device, require hybrid-joined device, require approved client app, require app protection policy, require Terms of Use, or require authentication strength.

Session controls apply after access is granted: sign-in frequency, persistent browser session, app-enforced restrictions (SharePoint/Exchange), Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps proxy, and continuous access evaluation.

Conditional Access replaces the legacy 'per-app MFA' and 'trusted IPs' settings. Those legacy mechanisms are still present but should be migrated to CA policies before they are deprecated. Use the CA migration guide in the Entra portal.
Controls whether the policy is active
Who the policy targets (include and exclude)
All users, specific users/groups, roles, guests, workload identities
All cloud apps, specific app IDs, user actions (register security info)
Any location, specific named locations, all trusted locations
Android, iOS, Windows, macOS, Linux, windowsPhone
low, medium, high, none (requires P2/ID Protection)
low, medium, high, none (requires P2/ID Protection)
What to require when conditions match
Block, MFA, compliantDevice, domainJoinedDevice, approvedApplication, authenticationStrength, termsOfUse
Creating a Policy via Graph API

CA policies are managed under /identity/conditionalAccess/policies. Creating a policy in the enabled state immediately affects sign-ins — always start with Report-Only mode or test in a non-production tenant first.

When a policy has multiple grant controls, the operator field determines whether all must be satisfied (AND) or any one suffices (OR). AND is more restrictive; OR is more flexible. For example, requiring MFA AND compliant device means users need both — useful for sensitive applications. Requiring MFA OR compliant device means a corporate device can bypass MFA.

'Require compliant device OR hybrid-joined device' is a common pattern for BYOD + corporate device environments. Corporate devices satisfy hybrid-join; BYOD devices satisfy Intune compliance enrollment.

Every CA policy should exclude at least one emergency access (break-glass) account or group. If a CA policy misconfiguration locks out all admins, break-glass accounts allow recovery without waiting for Microsoft Support. These accounts must be permanently excluded from all CA policies — including MFA requirements.
Report-Only Mode and the What-If Tool

Before enabling any new policy, set state to enabledForReportingButNotEnforcing (Report-Only). The policy is evaluated during every sign-in, and the result is recorded in the sign-in log under ConditionalAccessPolicies, but the grant/block decision is not enforced. Review Report-Only results for 7–14 days to confirm the policy targets the right users without unexpected blast radius.

// Query sign-in logs for Report-Only policy impact
// Find sign-ins where the policy would have blocked or required MFA
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| mv-expand Policy = parse_json(ConditionalAccessPolicies)
| where tostring(Policy.displayName) == 'Require MFA for Azure management'
| where tostring(Policy.result) in ('reportOnlySuccess', 'reportOnlyFailure', 'reportOnlyInterrupted')
| extend User   = UserPrincipalName
| extend Result = tostring(Policy.result)
| summarize Count=count() by User, Result
| order by Count desc

The What-If tool in the Entra admin center (Identity > Conditional Access > What If) lets you simulate a sign-in for a specific user, application, IP, device, and risk level, and shows which policies would fire and what the combined result would be. Use it to verify exclusions are working and to debug unexpected policy matches.

# List all CA policies with state and conditions summary
$policies = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET \
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/conditionalAccess/policies'

$policies.value | Select-Object displayName, state,
    @{N='Users';   E={ $_.conditions.users.includeUsers -join ',' }},
    @{N='Apps';    E={ $_.conditions.applications.includeApplications -join ',' }},
    @{N='Grant';   E={ $_.grantControls.builtInControls -join ',' }}

# Export all policies to JSON for IaC source-of-truth or audit snapshot
$policies.value | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 15 | Out-File ca-policies-snapshot-$(Get-Date -Format yyyyMMdd).json

# Check which policies include 'All users' and are in enabled state
$policies.value | Where-Object {
    $_.state -eq 'enabled' -and
    $_.conditions.users.includeUsers -contains 'All'
} | Select-Object displayName, state
Policy Targeting: Users, Groups, and Roles

User targeting in CA policies uses an include/exclude model. The most common patterns are: target All users with exclusions for specific groups, or target a specific security group. Targeting by directory role is supported — for example, targeting the Global Administrator role includes all Global Admins regardless of group membership.
All users, All apps, All client types
Baseline MFA for all interactive sign-ins
All users, All apps, clientAppTypes: exchangeActiveSync + other
Legacy protocols cannot satisfy MFA — block entirely
FIDO2 or certificate-based auth for privileged accounts
Enforce Intune compliance before M365 data access
All users, All apps, Sign-in risk: High
Immediately block high-risk sign-ins detected by ID Protection
All users, All apps, User risk: Medium+High
Force remediation for users with risky credentials
Extra gate before any ARM/portal action
All users, All apps, Location: not named trusted
Restrict sign-ins to corporate or trusted networks for sensitive apps

When a user reports unexpected MFA prompts, blocked access, or device compliance errors, the sign-in log is the primary diagnostic tool. Each sign-in record contains a ConditionalAccessPolicies array showing every policy evaluated, the result (success, failure, notApplied), and which grant controls were required.
CA policy requires MFA and user was not already MFA-satisfied in the session
A Block grant control matched — check ConditionalAccessPolicies for which policy blocked
User has not registered any MFA method yet; CA policy requires MFA
CA policy requires compliant device but the device has failed or not enrolled in Intune
CA policy requires hybrid-joined device but current device is not joined

Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE) is a session control that causes access tokens to be revoked in near-real-time when a risk event occurs — account disabled, password changed, sign-in session revoked — rather than waiting for the token's natural expiry (typically 1 hour). CAE requires both the client app and the Entra service to support it.

CA policies use an if-then model: conditions (user, app, location, device, risk) trigger controls (block, MFA, compliant device, ToU, session restrictions). All enabled matching policies are evaluated — Block always overrides Grant.
Always exclude a break-glass group from every CA policy. Without this exclusion, a misconfigured policy can lock all admins out of the tenant.
Deploy new policies in Report-Only state first. Use the What-If tool and query SigninLogs under ConditionalAccessPolicies to confirm the policy targets correctly before enabling.
Grant controls combine with AND (all required) or OR (any one suffices). Use AND for high-sensitivity resources; use OR for BYOD + corporate device environments.
Manage CA policies as code using Terraform (azuread_conditional_access_policy) or Bicep/ARM. Export JSON snapshots as a source-of-truth backup before making changes.

Session controls (sign-in frequency, persistent browser, MCAS proxy) apply after access is granted and control behavior within the authenticated session — independent of the grant controls that allowed access.
